title: Tactical Combat Casualty Care (TCCC) Overview
summary: The three-phase framework for managing trauma casualties when evacuation is delayed or unavailable.

steps.title: Phase 1 — Care Under Fire
steps.body: You are taking fire. The casualty is taking fire. The threat is immediate and active. Actions: Return fire. Take cover. Suppress the threat. Direct the casualty to return fire if able, or move to cover. Apply a tourniquet for life-threatening extremity bleeding — self-applied if able. Do NOT expose yourself to assess wounds. Do NOT move the casualty into the open. Threat elimination is the primary life-saving action at this phase.

steps.title: Phase 2 — Tactical Field Care
steps.body: The immediate threat has been neutralized or you are in a covered position with adequate time. Now you assess and treat. Use the MARCH protocol: M — Massive hemorrhage (tourniquets, wound packing). A — Airway (position, open airway, nasopharyngeal airway if available). R — Respiration (check for tension pneumothorax, seal open chest wounds). C — Circulation (treat shock, establish IV/IO access if trained). H — Hypothermia prevention (get the casualty off the ground, cover). This phase is where most life-saving interventions occur.

steps.title: Phase 3 — TACEVAC
steps.body: Tactical evacuation — the process of moving the casualty from the point of injury to a higher level of care. Actions: Reassess all interventions applied during Phases 1 and 2. Document what was done and when (time of tourniquet application is critical). Communicate casualty status to the evacuation team using the 9-Line MEDEVAC format or simplified status (immediate, delayed, minimal, expectant). Continue monitoring during transport — airway and bleeding status can change.

steps.title: MARCH Rapid Reference — Field Card
steps.body:
Commit this to memory or laminate a wallet card.

M — Massive hemorrhage: tourniquet, wound packing, junctional pressure.
A — Airway: chin lift, jaw thrust, NPA if available, recovery position.
R — Respiration: chest seal (both sides) for penetrating chest trauma; needle decompression for tension pneumo.
C — Circulation: establish IV/IO if trained; administer TXA within 3 hours of injury if available; treat hypothermia.
H — Hypothermia/head trauma: get casualty off cold ground; use space blanket; monitor neurological status.

Critical timelines: tourniquet within 60 seconds of arterial bleed; TXA within 180 minutes of injury; TACEVAC within the 'platinum hour' for penetrating trauma.

warnings: Uncontrolled hemorrhage is the leading cause of preventable combat death. Tourniquet application within seconds is more important than any other intervention.
TCCC is a framework — without training and repetition, execution under stress will be imperfect. Train with the tools you carry.
proTips: MARCH replaces the old ABC (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) in tactical medicine because Massive hemorrhage kills faster than airway compromise in trauma. Address bleeding first.
The military switched to MARCH from ABC specifically because data from combat operations showed that preventable deaths were overwhelmingly hemorrhagic. M comes first for a reason.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not attempt advanced interventions without prior TCCC training
Do not delay evacuation to perform procedures beyond your skill level

quickGlance.situation: You or someone with you has sustained a traumatic injury and professional medical evacuation is not immediately available
immediate: Eliminate or suppress active threat — move casualty to cover
Apply tourniquet to any life-threatening extremity bleeding immediately
Use MARCH protocol: Massive hemorrhage → Airway → Respiration → Circulation → Hypothermia
then: Document all interventions with time applied
Package casualty for TACEVAC — note tourniquet application time on forehead

title: Tourniquet Application
summary: Apply a tourniquet correctly — including one-handed self-application — for life-threatening extremity bleeding.

steps.title: When to apply
steps.body: Apply a tourniquet when: bleeding from an extremity cannot be controlled with direct pressure; bleeding is pulsatile (bright red, spurting — arterial); the wound is large or complex with multiple bleeding points; the casualty is losing consciousness from blood loss. If in doubt, apply the tourniquet. Tourniquet complications (nerve damage, tissue death) are extremely rare at treatment durations under 2 hours and are always preferable to hemorrhagic death.

steps.title: Placement
steps.body: Apply 2–3 inches above the wound, NOT over a joint. For a wound below the knee or elbow, apply at mid-thigh or mid-upper arm ('high and tight' is the military standard for severe wounds — apply as high as possible on the limb). Place over clothing if necessary — do not waste time removing clothing in an emergency.

steps.title: Application with a CAT or SOFTT-W
steps.body: Slide the limb through the loop. Pull the free end of the strap tight — as tight as you can manually before engaging the windlass. Twist the windlass rod until bleeding stops completely — not just reduces. Lock the rod in the retention strap or clip. The casualty will experience significant pain — this is expected and indicates proper application. Note the time on the tourniquet or the casualty's forehead.

steps.title: One-handed self-application — arm
steps.body: Lay the tourniquet on a flat surface or your leg. Insert the injured arm through the loop. Use your other hand and your teeth to pull the strap tight. Use your good hand to operate the windlass. Bite and hold the windlass if needed while locking.

steps.title: One-handed self-application — leg
steps.body: Loop tourniquet around the thigh. Pull the strap through the buckle with your good hand as tight as possible. Use your knee, a surface, or a tree to help lever the strap tight. Operate the windlass with your good hand. Twist until bleeding stops. Note the time.

steps.title: Improvised tourniquet
steps.body: If no commercial tourniquet: use a cloth strip 2+ inches wide (narrower cuts into tissue and is less effective). Form a loop and tie around the limb. Insert a rigid rod (stick, pen, screwdriver) through the loop and twist until bleeding stops. Lock the rod against the limb with another wrap of cloth. Improvised tourniquets have higher failure rates than commercial devices — use as last resort only.

warnings: Never remove a tourniquet in the field once applied — re-application or conversion can be performed at a higher level of care with resuscitation capability.
A tourniquet that does not stop bleeding is either improperly applied (not tight enough) or requires a second tourniquet applied proximal to (above) the first.
Do not pad under a tourniquet — it reduces effectiveness.
proTips: The rule of thumb: when you stop twisting the windlass, check if you can see pulsatile bleeding. If yes, keep twisting. If the bleeding has stopped, you're done. Do not 'just a little more' — that's the right tension.
Carry at minimum two tourniquets. Junctional hemorrhage (groin, armpit, neck) cannot be controlled with a limb tourniquet — have additional pressure dressings for these wounds.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not apply tourniquet for venous (slow, dark) bleeding that can be controlled with direct pressure
Do not apply over a joint — position 2-3 inches above wound
Do not remove tourniquet in the field if applied for arterial bleed — removal must be done by trained medical personnel

quickGlance.situation: You or someone with you has life-threatening arterial bleeding from an extremity — bright red blood pumping or spurting
immediate: Position tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound on the limb, not over clothing if avoidable
Tighten windlass until bleeding stops completely — not just slows
Note and mark the time of tourniquet application — write on patient's forehead if needed
then: Monitor for renewed bleeding around or below tourniquet
Do not remove — await trained medical personnel for conversion

title: Wound Packing
summary: Pack wounds with gauze or improvised materials to control hemorrhage that cannot be tourniqueted.

steps.title: When to pack
steps.body: Pack wounds that: cannot receive a tourniquet (junctional wounds — neck, groin, axilla/armpit); are in the torso; are large complex wounds with multiple bleeding points. Wound packing is the primary intervention for junctional hemorrhage, which is the leading cause of preventable deaths that reach higher care.

steps.title: Prepare the wound
steps.body: If bleeding is visible, use your fingers to identify the source — often deep in the wound cavity, not at the surface. Controlling surface bleeding while deep bleeding continues is a critical failure. Pack to the source, not the surface. Do not remove impaled objects — pack around them.

steps.title: Pack with hemostatic gauze
steps.body: If you have combat gauze (QuikClot, Celox, HemCon): begin packing directly into the deepest point of the wound, against the bleeding source. Pack in a systematic figure-eight or accordion fold — do not bunch it randomly. Fill the wound cavity completely.

steps.title: Pack with improvised material
steps.body: If no hemostatic gauze: use the cleanest cloth available — improvised dressing from a t-shirt, bandana, or any absorbent material. Tear into strips if needed. Pack into the wound the same way — to the deepest point, filling the cavity. The packing material is less important than the application technique.

steps.title: Apply pressure
steps.body: Once the wound is packed full, apply firm, continuous direct pressure with both hands using bodyweight for 3 minutes with commercial hemostatic gauze, 10 minutes with improvised packing. This is not gentle pressure — it is maximum sustained pressure. Do not lift your hands to check during this time.

steps.title: Secure the packing
steps.body: After direct pressure, apply a pressure bandage over the wound to maintain pressure. Improvise with cloth strips tied firmly. The goal is to maintain the pressure you just applied. Check every few minutes — bleeding through a saturated dressing means add more dressing on top and continue pressure. Do not remove the original packing.

warnings: Wound packing is painful. A conscious casualty will resist. Communicate what you are doing and why. If the casualty is unconscious, ensure airway is maintained as you work.
Never pack an open chest wound (penetrating chest wound that makes sucking noise) — see medical-005 for chest wound management. Packing a tension pneumothorax can be fatal.
proTips: Junctional hemorrhage (neck, groin, armpit) is the leading cause of deaths that reach care but die before surgery. These wounds bleed out in minutes from major vessels. Pack aggressively and immediately.

QuikClot Z-fold combat gauze is the TCCC-recommended hemostatic agent. Carry it. The civilian equivalent (QuikClot Advanced Clotting Gauze) is available without prescription and should be in every kit.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not pack wounds to the chest, neck, or abdomen — pack only for extremity and junctional wounds
Do not remove packing material once applied — re-packing without proper training can restart hemorrhage

quickGlance.situation: You have a severe wound with uncontrolled hemorrhage that cannot be controlled with a tourniquet (junctional wound, neck, groin)
immediate: Apply hemostatic gauze (QuikClot/Celox) — pack it deep into the wound cavity, not just on the surface
Apply firm direct pressure to the packed wound with full body weight for minimum 3 minutes
Secure with a pressure bandage to maintain compression
then: Monitor for bleeding through the dressing — if soaking through, add more pressure on top
Do not remove the packing in the field

title: Junctional Hemorrhage Control
summary: Control life-threatening bleeding at the neck, groin, and armpit where tourniquets cannot be applied.

steps.title: Recognize junctional hemorrhage
steps.body: Junctional zones are where limbs attach to the body: the inguinal fold (groin), the axilla (armpit), and the neck. Major vascular structures in these zones — femoral artery (groin), axillary artery (armpit), carotid/jugular (neck) — can produce fatal hemorrhage in under 3 minutes. A tourniquet cannot be applied high enough to control these wounds.

steps.title: Groin hemorrhage
steps.body: Apply firm bimanual pressure directly over the inguinal fold (where the leg meets the body). If trained and junctional tourniquet device (JETT, iTClamp, AAJT) is available, apply per training. Otherwise, pack the wound deeply with hemostatic gauze and apply maximum sustained direct pressure with bodyweight. Position: stand or kneel over the casualty, press both hands into the wound, lock your elbows and transfer your weight.

steps.title: Axillary hemorrhage (armpit)
steps.body: Pack the axilla deeply with hemostatic gauze. Have an assistant or improvised pressure device maintain sustained pressure. Arm compression: fold the arm across the chest and apply a pressure wrap from axilla over shoulder and under the opposite axilla — this compresses the wound. Elevate the arm if possible.

steps.title: Neck hemorrhage
steps.body: Penetrating neck wounds with arterial involvement are extremely time-critical. Pack the wound firmly with hemostatic gauze. Apply sustained manual pressure UNILATERALLY — do not apply circumferential pressure around the neck (occludes both carotids and the airway). Pressure on one side only. Maintain airway — blood in the airway can cause rapid death from asphyxiation independently of blood loss.

warnings: Neck wounds with arterial bleeding are surgical emergencies. Field management buys minutes, not hours. Aggressive evacuation is essential.
Do not remove your hands from a packing site to 'check progress' — you will lose the clot forming beneath.
proTips: The iTClamp is a bridge device for neck hemorrhage — it clamps skin around the wound and creates an enclosed hematoma that tamponades the bleeding. It is a legitimate field device and EMTs and combat medics carry it. For civilian kits, it is available and worth considering.
Bilateral femoral artery wounds (both groin areas) are almost universally fatal without surgical capability. Understand the limits of field medicine.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not attempt to control junctional hemorrhage without hemostatic gauze — improvised packing is a last resort only
Do not apply circumferential pressure around the neck — compress one side only to avoid airway occlusion

quickGlance.situation: Life-threatening bleeding at the neck, groin, or armpit where a tourniquet cannot be applied
immediate: Pack the wound deeply with hemostatic gauze — do not just apply to the surface
Apply maximum sustained direct pressure with both hands using bodyweight for minimum 3 minutes
For groin: stand or kneel over the casualty, lock elbows, and lean in
then: Maintain pressure continuously — do not lift hands to check until 3 minutes have elapsed
Secure packing and maintain pressure during evacuation

title: Tension Pneumothorax Recognition and Treatment
summary: Recognize and treat a tension pneumothorax — a rapidly fatal chest condition with a simple field intervention.

steps.title: Understand the mechanism
steps.body: A tension pneumothorax occurs when air accumulates in the pleural space (around the lung) without a means to escape. Each breath forces more air in, progressively collapsing the lung and eventually compressing the heart, causing obstructive shock and death. This can occur from penetrating chest trauma, blunt trauma, or occasionally from an improperly sealed chest wound.

steps.title: Recognize the signs
steps.body: Signs of tension pneumothorax: penetrating or blunt chest trauma mechanism; increasing respiratory distress and tachycardia; absent or diminished breath sounds on the affected side (difficult to assess in field); trachea deviated away from the affected side (late sign); hypotension that does not respond to fluids; distended neck veins (JVD) — late sign. The casualty in tension PTX deteriorates rapidly — shock, unconsciousness, death follow in minutes without treatment.

steps.title: Seal open chest wounds first
steps.body: Open (sucking) chest wounds — penetrating chest wounds that make a sucking or bubbling sound with breathing — must be sealed. Apply a vented chest seal (Halo, Russell) or improvise with a square of plastic secured on three sides (the fourth side acts as a flutter valve, allowing air out but not in). If the casualty deteriorates after sealing, consider that the seal has created a tension PTX — burp the seal by lifting one edge.

steps.title: Needle decompression — indication
steps.body: Perform needle decompression when: the casualty has penetrating or severe blunt chest trauma AND is in respiratory distress and shock AND breath sounds are absent on one side. In a grid-down scenario without advanced assessment tools, treat the side of the wound (or the side of absent/diminished breath sounds). A tension PTX is fatal without decompression — the intervention is life-saving.

steps.title: Needle decompression — procedure
steps.body: Site: Second intercostal space (ICS), midclavicular line (MCL) on the affected side. Find the clavicle — go 2 finger-widths below the clavicle, directly over the nipple line. Alternative site (used in TCCC): 4th or 5th ICS, anterior axillary line (on the side of the chest, above and forward of the armpit). Insert a 14-gauge needle/catheter (3.25 inches or longer — standard IV needles are often too short to penetrate the chest wall). Insert perpendicular to the chest wall, over the top of the rib (going under the rib risks the neurovascular bundle). A rush of air confirms decompression. Leave the catheter in place or remove the needle over the catheter.

warnings: Needle decompression is an invasive procedure. Performing it on a patient without tension PTX will cause a simple pneumothorax — an injury, but survivable. Not performing it on a patient with tension PTX is fatal. When the indication is met, err on the side of action.
Standard 14g 3.25-inch needles may not reach the pleural space in heavy-set individuals. Use the longest available needle.
proTips: The anterior axillary line (4th/5th ICS) site is increasingly preferred in TCCC because it is more reliably effective for penetrating the chest wall and less likely to fail due to body habitus.
Carry a purpose-built chest decompression needle (Tactical Medical Solutions, North American Rescue) — they are pre-attached to a large-bore catheter at the appropriate length.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not perform needle decompression without a confirmed or strongly suspected tension pneumothorax — the procedure creates a pneumothorax if one is not present
Do not seal an open chest wound completely (no flutter valve) — a fully sealed chest wound can cause or worsen tension pneumothorax

quickGlance.situation: Patient has penetrating or severe blunt chest trauma with respiratory distress, shock, and absent breath sounds on one side
immediate: Seal any open (sucking) chest wound with a vented chest seal or three-sided improvised seal
If patient deteriorates after sealing, burp the seal to release trapped air
If tension PTX confirmed: needle decompression at 2nd ICS midclavicular line or 4th/5th ICS anterior axillary line
then: Monitor respiratory status continuously after decompression
Reassess for recurrence — a second decompression may be required

title: Shock — Recognition and Management
summary: Recognize shock early and apply the field interventions that improve survival.

steps.title: Types of shock
steps.body: Hemorrhagic shock (most common in trauma): caused by blood loss. Treat by controlling bleeding. Neurogenic shock: spinal cord injury causes vessel dilation. Septic shock: systemic infection. Obstructive shock: tension PTX, cardiac tamponade. Anaphylactic shock: severe allergic reaction — treat with epinephrine. In a trauma scenario, assume hemorrhagic shock until proven otherwise.

steps.title: Early signs
steps.body: Heart rate >100 bpm; respiratory rate >20; skin pale, cool, clammy; anxiety, restlessness, confusion (brain is poorly perfused); pulse is rapid and weak at wrist (radial); capillary refill >2 seconds. These signs appear when approximately 15–30% of blood volume is lost. The body compensates well initially — do not be reassured by a casualty who appears 'okay' after significant blood loss.

steps.title: Late signs — decompensated shock
steps.body: Blood pressure drops (systolic <90 mmHg); pulse is absent at wrist, present at carotid only; skin mottled and pale; decreased consciousness or unconsciousness; shallow, irregular breathing. Decompensated shock means compensatory mechanisms have failed. Death follows rapidly without intervention. Loss of radial pulse = approximately 50% blood volume lost.

steps.title: Field treatment
steps.body: Stop the bleeding (tourniquets, wound packing, pressure). Position: lay flat, elevate the legs 12 inches if no suspected spinal injury — this shifts blood volume toward the core and brain. Keep warm — hypothermia worsens coagulopathy (the blood's ability to clot). If IV access is available and you are trained, administer IV fluids — permissive hypotension (systolic 80–90) is acceptable in hemorrhagic shock; aggressive fluid resuscitation worsens outcomes.

warnings: Oral fluids are contraindicated in a patient with altered mental status (aspiration risk) and may be contraindicated before surgical intervention depending on the wound type.
Hypothermia is a major complication of shock — the 'lethal triad' of trauma is hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy. All three worsen each other. Keep the patient warm.
proTips: The radial pulse check is your rapid field shock assessment tool: if you can feel a pulse at the wrist, systolic BP is approximately >80. If the radial pulse is absent but the carotid is present, systolic is approximately 60–80. No carotid: <60.

Jelly belly sign: in a supine patient, the abdomen becomes visibly distended and board-hard in internal hemorrhage. This indicates intra-abdominal bleeding requiring surgery — no field intervention stops this. Rapid evacuation is the only intervention.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not administer medications if the person has known allergies or you are uncertain
Do not move a trauma patient with suspected spinal injury unless immediate danger requires it

quickGlance.situation: A person is showing signs of hemorrhagic shock: pale/grey skin, rapid weak pulse, confusion, excessive thirst, cold clammy extremities
immediate: Control any identified bleeding source immediately — tourniquet or wound pack
Lay the casualty flat and elevate legs 6-12 inches if no spinal injury suspected
Prevent heat loss — cover with blankets or anything available
then: Monitor pulse and mental status every 5 minutes
Keep casualty warm — hypothermia accelerates shock progression

title: Airway Management Without Equipment
summary: Maintain a patent airway in an unconscious casualty using position and manual techniques.

steps.title: Assessment
steps.body: Look: is the chest rising? Listen: air movement at the mouth and nose? Feel: airflow on your cheek? If no airflow, the airway is obstructed. Common causes: tongue (most common in unconscious patient), blood/vomit, foreign object, swelling, or jaw trauma.

steps.title: Head-tilt chin-lift
steps.body: Place one hand on the forehead and push backward while lifting the chin with two fingers of the other hand. This extends the neck, lifting the tongue off the posterior pharynx and opening the airway. Do NOT use this technique if cervical spine injury is suspected — use jaw thrust instead.

steps.title: Jaw thrust (spine precaution)
steps.body: Position behind the casualty's head. Place fingers behind the angles of the jaw on both sides. Push the jaw forward and upward without moving the neck. This opens the airway while maintaining cervical spine neutrality.

steps.title: Recovery position
steps.body: In an unconscious breathing casualty with no suspected spinal injury, the recovery position (lateral recumbent) is the safest maintenance position. Roll the casualty onto their side, top knee bent forward, top arm supporting the head. This prevents aspiration of blood, vomit, and secretions and prevents the tongue from obstructing the airway without requiring ongoing manual support.

steps.title: Finger sweep
steps.body: If you can visualize an obstruction in the mouth (blood, vomit, dislodged teeth, food), remove it with a gloved finger sweep. Do not perform blind finger sweeps — pushing an obstruction deeper is the risk. Only remove what you can see.

steps.title: Nasopharyngeal airway (NPA)
steps.body: If an NPA is available: measure from the nostril to the earlobe for sizing. Lubricate with whatever is available (petroleum jelly, lip balm, saliva). Insert into the right nostril (the one with the bevel facing the septum), advancing gently along the floor of the nasal cavity. The NPA maintains airway without the need for the patient's cooperation and works in patients with clenched jaws or dental damage.

warnings: Airway compromise and hemorrhage must be managed simultaneously in trauma. Do not complete all airway steps before controlling an arterial bleed — MARCH applies.
If the airway is maintained with a maneuver and the patient vomits, roll them immediately to the recovery position to clear the airway.
proTips: A comatose trauma patient should be maintained in the recovery position for transport whenever possible — this single position change prevents more aspiration deaths than any other airway intervention.

Carry a NPA in your medical kit. A size 28 French NPA fits the majority of adults. They cost a few dollars, weigh almost nothing, and have saved lives in scenarios where a surgical airway would otherwise be required.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not attempt nasopharyngeal airway on a patient with facial fractures or suspected basilar skull fracture
Do not use a finger sweep on an unconscious patient without directly visualizing the obstruction

quickGlance.situation: A person is unconscious or has compromised airway — gurgling, stridor, inability to speak, or apnea
immediate: Tilt head back and lift chin to open the airway
Look, listen, and feel for breathing — assess for 10 seconds
If no breathing and trained: start rescue breaths; if not trained: position in recovery position
then: Insert nasopharyngeal airway if available and patient is unconscious without facial trauma
Monitor airway continuously — position changes can compromise it

title: Oral Rehydration Therapy
summary: Treat severe dehydration and diarrheal illness with Oral Rehydration Salts — more effective than plain water.

steps.title: When ORT is required
steps.body: Signs of severe dehydration: sunken eyes, extremely dry mouth and mucous membranes, decreased or absent urination, skin 'tenting' (pinch skin on back of hand — slow return to normal takes >2 seconds), confusion, weakness, inability to stand. In cholera and severe gastroenteritis, plain water replacement is insufficient — electrolytes are lost faster than water and plain water can paradoxically worsen cellular dehydration.

steps.title: WHO ORS formula
steps.body: In 1 liter of clean (purified) water, dissolve: 6 level teaspoons of sugar (glucose or sucrose) AND 1/2 teaspoon of salt (sodium chloride). Stir completely until dissolved. This is the WHO standard ORS formulation. It should taste mildly salty and sweet — not strongly either. Too much salt is dangerous; measure carefully.

steps.title: Administration
steps.body: Give small, frequent sips — not large volumes rapidly. In an adult: 200–400mL per hour for the first 4 hours, then as much as tolerated. In a child under 2: 50–100mL after each loose stool. The patient should show improvement (increased urination, improved skin turgor, improved alertness) within 4–6 hours of ORT.

steps.title: Continue feeding
steps.body: Do not fast a patient with diarrhea. The gut can still absorb nutrients even during diarrheal illness. Bland, easily digestible foods (rice, plain crackers, bananas, boiled potato) are appropriate. Avoid dairy, fats, and high-fiber foods during acute illness. BRAT diet (bananas, rice, applesauce, toast) if available.

warnings: If vomiting prevents ORT administration, small sips every 5 minutes often succeed where larger volumes fail. Wait 10 minutes after a vomiting episode before resuming.
Bloody diarrhea (dysentery) with fever indicates a bacterial infection and requires antibiotics if available. ORT is still required but is not sufficient treatment alone.
ORT does not treat the cause of diarrhea — it prevents death from dehydration while the illness runs its course.
proTips: Pre-packaged ORS packets (Pedialyte sachets, DripDrop, oral rehydration sachets) are compact, lightweight, shelf-stable, and far more reliable than field-mixed solutions. Stock them in your medical kit.
Coconut water contains natural electrolytes and serves as a reasonable ORS substitute in regions where coconuts are available.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not give ORT rapidly in large volumes — small sips only to avoid triggering vomiting
Do not substitute plain water alone for ORS in severe diarrheal illness — electrolyte replacement is essential

quickGlance.situation: A person shows signs of severe dehydration: sunken eyes, dry mouth, decreased urination, confusion, or weakness
immediate: Mix WHO ORS: 1 liter clean water + 6 teaspoons sugar + 1/2 teaspoon salt
Give small sips continuously — 200-400mL per hour for adults
Monitor for improvement: increased urination, improved alertness within 4-6 hours
then: Continue ORT until dehydration signs resolve
Introduce bland foods as tolerated — do not fast the patient

title: Field Sanitation and Latrine Construction
summary: Establish field sanitation systems that prevent the diseases that kill more people in disaster than trauma.

steps.title: Why sanitation is survival-critical
steps.body: In a grid-down or disaster scenario, improper waste disposal is the primary driver of cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A, and dysentery outbreaks. These diseases killed more soldiers in every 20th century conflict than enemy fire until modern field sanitation was mandated. In a population without sanitation, waterborne disease outbreaks can kill 10–30% of an affected group within weeks.

steps.title: Cat hole latrines (individual, short-term)
steps.body: For individuals or small groups over 1–3 days: dig holes 6–8 inches deep, at least 200 feet from water sources, camps, and trails. Defecate in the hole, cover with the excavated soil, and pack down. This puts waste in the biologically active layer of soil where decomposition occurs fastest. Mark used holes with a stick.

steps.title: Pit latrine (group, extended operations)
steps.body: For groups or extended operations: dig a trench 2–4 feet deep, 1 foot wide, as long as necessary. Site it downhill and downwind from the camp, at least 200 feet from any water source. Build a straddle-trench design — users straddle the trench. Cover the opening with soil after each use. When the trench is 3/4 full, fill completely, mark the site, and relocate.

steps.title: Hand hygiene enforcement
steps.body: Establish a hand-washing station at the latrine exit — water container with a hole or valve, soap or ash (ash is an effective soap substitute), and a drying towel. Make hand-washing mandatory, not optional, within the group. The single most effective disease-prevention measure in any field environment is hand hygiene after defecation and before food preparation.

steps.title: Waste water and gray water
steps.body: Gray water (cooking, washing) must also be disposed of properly. Dig a sump pit — 1-2 feet deep, filled with gravel or rocks — at least 200 feet from water sources. Pour gray water into the sump pit, which filters it through the soil. Do not dispose of gray water in running water sources.

warnings: Burning waste is preferable to open defecation but produces toxic fumes — never burn waste in enclosed spaces.
Do not site a latrine uphill from any water source. Groundwater flows downhill. Contamination of a water source from a poorly sited latrine can affect everyone downstream.
proTips: Lime (calcium hydroxide) is the traditional field sanitation agent. Dusted over latrine contents, it deactivates pathogens, reduces odor, and accelerates decomposition. Pool shock (calcium hypochlorite) can substitute.
The F-diagram: Flies, Fingers, Fields, Fluids, Fomites, Food — the six fecal-oral transmission routes. Interrupting any route breaks the chain of disease transmission.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not site latrines within 200 feet of any water source — groundwater contamination can affect the entire group
Do not burn waste in enclosed spaces — toxic fumes pose serious respiratory risk

quickGlance.situation: Your group needs to establish sanitation in a field or grid-down environment for more than 24 hours
immediate: Designate a latrine site at least 200 feet downhill and downwind from camp and water sources
Dig cat holes (6-8 inches deep) for short-term or pit trench (2-4 feet deep) for group use
Set up a hand-washing station at the latrine exit with water and soap or ash
then: Enforce mandatory hand-washing after every use
Relocate latrine when trench is 3/4 full — mark used sites clearly

title: Vector Control — Mosquitoes, Ticks, and Rodents
summary: Reduce vector-borne disease transmission through site selection, barriers, and environmental control.

steps.title: Mosquito control
steps.body: Site camps away from standing water — mosquitoes breed in any standing water, including puddles, containers, and tire tracks. Drain, cover, or treat all water containers not intended for drinking. Sleep under a bed net (impregnated with permethrin is best). Apply DEET or permethrin to exposed skin and clothing. In high-malaria areas, chemoprophylaxis (doxycycline, mefloquine) is the only reliable prevention — field measures reduce but do not eliminate risk.

steps.title: Tick prevention
steps.body: Wear long sleeves and pants tucked into boots in tick habitat (tall grass, brush, wooded areas). Treat clothing with permethrin. Conduct full body tick checks every evening — key areas: scalp, behind ears, back of neck, armpits, groin, behind knees. A tick must be attached for 24–36 hours to transmit Lyme disease — early removal is highly protective.

steps.title: Tick removal
steps.body: Use fine-tipped tweezers or a tick removal tool. Grasp the tick as close to the skin surface as possible. Pull upward with steady, even pressure — do not twist or jerk. Do not use petroleum jelly, nail polish, heat, or other methods — these increase disease transmission by agitating the tick. Clean the bite site with alcohol or soap and water after removal.

steps.title: Rodent control
steps.body: Rodents carry hantavirus, plague, typhus, leptospirosis, and numerous other serious pathogens. Do not leave food accessible to rodents — store in sealed containers off the ground. Do not sleep near rodent burrows or nesting sites. Do not handle dead rodents without gloves. If sleeping in structures with rodent evidence, clean with bleach solution and ventilate before occupying.

warnings: Hantavirus: Do not sweep or vacuum rodent droppings dry — the virus is airborne. Wet droppings with bleach solution (1:10 bleach to water) and allow to soak before wiping up with gloves.
In malaria-endemic areas, a single night without mosquito protection can result in infection. In grid-down tropical scenarios, malaria is a major threat.
proTips: Permethrin-treated clothing remains effective through 6+ washes and provides significantly better protection than topical repellents alone. Treat your field clothing in advance of deployment.
Natural mosquito repellents (citronella, lemon eucalyptus oil — OLE specifically) have evidence-based efficacy but must be reapplied every 30–60 minutes versus 4–6 hours for DEET.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not handle dead rodents without gloves — hantavirus and plague are transmitted through contact and aerosolized droppings
Do not sweep or vacuum dry rodent droppings — wet with bleach solution first to prevent aerosolization

quickGlance.situation: Operating in an environment with mosquitoes, ticks, or rodent presence that poses disease transmission risk
immediate: Apply DEET to exposed skin and permethrin to clothing
Tuck pants into boots and wear long sleeves in tick habitat
Eliminate or avoid standing water near camp to reduce mosquito breeding
then: Conduct full-body tick check every evening — especially scalp, armpits, groin, and behind knees
Store all food in sealed containers off the ground to deter rodents

title: Food Safety and Spoilage Identification
summary: Identify spoiled food, understand safe handling without refrigeration, and prevent food-borne illness.

steps.title: The temperature danger zone
steps.body: Bacteria multiply rapidly between 40°F and 140°F (4°C–60°C). Without refrigeration, all perishable foods enter this zone. Cooked food left at ambient temperature in warm weather becomes dangerous within 2–4 hours. In a grid-down scenario with no refrigeration, cook only what you will eat immediately and preserve the rest (smoke, salt, dry).

steps.title: Identify spoilage — smell
steps.body: The most reliable field test. Any off, sour, or ammonia-like smell in meat or poultry indicates spoilage. Fish smells strongly of ammonia when spoiled (vs. mild sea smell when fresh). Dairy products smell sour and sometimes putrid. Trust your nose — the human nose evolved specifically to detect rotting protein.

steps.title: Identify spoilage — visual
steps.body: Slime on meat or fish surfaces indicates bacterial growth. Mold on bread, cheese, and grains — visible green, blue, black, or white fuzzy growth. Discoloration in meat (gray, green, or brown patches in normally red meat). Bloated canned or sealed food — gas production from bacteria or botulism. Never eat from a bloated can.

steps.title: Botulism risk
steps.body: Clostridium botulinum produces toxin in low-oxygen, low-acid, moderate-temperature environments — precisely the conditions in home-canned or improperly preserved food. Botulism toxin is one of the most lethal substances known. Signs: descending paralysis (starts with blurred vision, drooping eyelids, difficulty swallowing, then progresses to paralysis and respiratory failure). If suspected, there is no field treatment — only supportive care and antitoxin (hospital). Never take risks with canned or preserved food showing any suspect signs.

warnings: Do not apply the rule 'if it looks okay and smells okay, it's okay' universally — Salmonella, E. coli O157:H7, and Norovirus produce no detectable smell or appearance changes.
Food-borne illness in a grid-down scenario with dehydration risk can be fatal — it is not simply 'food poisoning' that will pass. It can trigger dehydration severe enough to require ORT or IV fluids.
proTips: High-acid foods (vinegar-preserved, citrus-pickled) are significantly more resistant to spoilage. Vinegar pickling is a viable field preservation technique for vegetables.

The nose blind phenomenon: people who are used to being around spoiled food become desensitized. In a group, have different people smell-test food if possible — fresh noses detect spoilage that habituated noses miss.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not eat from a bloated or swollen can — this is a sign of botulism toxin production and is potentially lethal
Do not rely solely on smell or appearance to determine food safety — many pathogens (Salmonella, E. coli, Norovirus) are odorless and invisible

quickGlance.situation: You need to assess whether food is safe to eat without refrigeration or when uncertain about storage conditions
immediate: Inspect for bloating, slime, unusual color, or mold — discard any food showing these signs
Smell test: off, sour, or ammonia odor in meat or fish indicates spoilage
Never eat from a bloated can — discard it without opening
then: Cook only what will be consumed immediately — do not store cooked food without refrigeration in warm weather
Prioritize high-acid or properly preserved foods (vinegar-pickled, dried, smoked) over fresh perishables

title: Combat Stress Reaction — Recognition and Management
summary: Recognize combat stress reaction and apply BICEPS protocol to return personnel to function.

steps.title: What is combat stress reaction
steps.body: Combat Stress Reaction (CSR) — also called battle fatigue or acute stress reaction — is a predictable, normal response to abnormal levels of physical and psychological stress. It is not a sign of weakness or psychiatric disorder. Under sufficient stress, any person can experience CSR. It is distinct from PTSD (which is a longer-term condition) and is typically reversible with proper immediate management.

steps.title: Signs of CSR
steps.body: Trembling, shaking, startle response to minor stimuli. Confusion, poor decision-making, difficulty following instructions. Hypervigilance or, conversely, unresponsiveness/dissociation. Weeping or inappropriate emotional responses. Physical complaints without apparent physical cause (headache, GI symptoms, fatigue). Freezing under fire or in emergency action.

steps.title: BICEPS protocol
steps.body: B — Brevity: intervention should last hours to days, not weeks. The goal is rapid return to function. I — Immediacy: address CSR immediately; delay worsens outcomes. C — Centrality: treat near the unit/group, not evacuated far away — this reinforces identity and belonging. E — Expectancy: explicitly tell the person they are normal, they will recover, and they will return to the group. P — Proximity: keep them close to their group, not isolated. S — Simplicity: basic needs — sleep, food, water, safety, and a trusted person to talk to.

steps.title: Practical intervention
steps.body: Remove from the immediate stressor if possible. Ensure the person is physically safe, warm, and hydrated. Allow them to talk if they want to — do not force a debrief. Do not permit the person to isolate. Assign a simple, low-complexity task to restore sense of purpose and competence. Maintain normalcy of routine — meals, rest schedule.

warnings: Do not dismiss or shame CSR. Phrases like 'man up' or 'others have it worse' worsen outcomes and damage trust. Acknowledge the stress is real and the response is normal.
A person who has experienced CSR may have delayed reaction to trauma weeks later (PTSD). Be aware that initial recovery does not mean the person is fully processed.
proTips: Sleep deprivation is the single largest contributing factor to CSR. A person who has not slept in 36+ hours has measurably impaired judgment equivalent to intoxication. Prioritize rotation schedules that allow minimum 4-hour sleep blocks.

Unit cohesion (strong interpersonal bonds within the group) is the most protective factor against CSR. Groups that trust each other, train together, and communicate openly have dramatically lower CSR rates under equivalent stress.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not dismiss or shame combat stress reaction — phrases like 'man up' worsen outcomes and damage unit trust
Do not isolate a person experiencing CSR — proximity to the group is a core part of treatment

quickGlance.situation: A person in your group is showing signs of combat stress reaction: trembling, confusion, freezing, unresponsiveness, or inappropriate emotional response under stress
immediate: Remove the person from the immediate stressor if tactically possible
Ensure they are physically safe, warm, fed, and hydrated
Tell them explicitly: their response is normal, they will recover, and they are not alone
then: Keep them near the group — do not evacuate or isolate unless medically necessary
Assign a simple, low-stakes task to restore sense of competence and purpose

title: Long-Term Patient Care Without Resupply
summary: Manage a sick or injured person in an extended grid-down scenario without professional medical care or resupply.

steps.title: Establish a sick bay
steps.body: Designate a specific area for patient care — separate from food preparation and the general camp if possible. The area should be: sheltered from weather, easy to access for a caregiver, equipped with the cleanest available bedding, positioned to allow monitoring without continuous attendance. A sick person in a cold, wet, or exposed position will deteriorate rapidly regardless of other interventions.

steps.title: Monitoring and documentation
steps.body: Record vitals at least every 4 hours: heart rate (normal 60–100), respiratory rate (normal 12–20), temperature (estimate with hand to forehead if no thermometer), mental status (alert, confused, unconscious), urine output (adequate hydration = urine produced every 4–6 hours, pale yellow). Write it down — memory of trends is unreliable under stress.

steps.title: Fluid and nutrition management
steps.body: An ill person who cannot eat must still hydrate. Clear fluids (water, ORS, clear broth) every 30–60 minutes. As tolerance improves, progress to soft, easily digestible foods. A person with a fever loses significantly more fluid through sweating and respiration — increase fluid intake proportionally. Caloric deficit is survivable for weeks; dehydration is fatal in days.

steps.title: Wound care and infection prevention
steps.body: Clean wounds daily with the cleanest water available (boiled and cooled is best). Inspect for signs of infection: increasing redness, warmth, swelling, pus, red streaks extending from the wound (lymphangitis — indicates spreading infection requiring antibiotics if available). Change dressings when saturated or soiled. Keep wounds covered to prevent further contamination.

steps.title: Caregiver sustainability
steps.body: Extended patient care is exhausting. A single caregiver who does not sleep, eat, or maintain their own health will become a second patient. Establish rotation schedules. Define clear task handoff — what was given, when, what was observed. Caregiver fatigue causes medication errors, missed deterioration signs, and failure to maintain hygiene protocols.

warnings: Antibiotic use without diagnosis and appropriate selection drives antibiotic resistance and can cause significant adverse effects. Use antibiotics from your kit only when infection signs are clear and significant.
Pain management without opioids: ibuprofen (anti-inflammatory and analgesic) and acetaminophen taken together provide additive pain relief. Both should be in your medical kit in abundance.
proTips: Honey is a legitimate wound care agent with documented antimicrobial properties. Medical-grade Manuka honey is preferred but any raw honey can be applied to clean wounds as a topical antimicrobial in the absence of commercial dressings.

Maggot debridement is effective and has been used in field medicine for centuries — sterile fly larvae (blowfly maggots, specifically Phaenicia sericata) clean necrotic tissue from wounds more effectively than many surgical debridement methods. This is a last-resort but evidence-based technique.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not use antibiotics without clear signs of infection — inappropriate use drives resistance and can cause adverse effects
Do not allow a single caregiver to provide continuous care without rotation — caregiver fatigue causes errors and the caregiver becomes a second patient

quickGlance.situation: You are managing a sick or injured person in a grid-down scenario without access to professional medical care or resupply
immediate: Designate a sheltered, clean area for the patient — separate from food prep and the general camp
Establish a monitoring schedule: record heart rate, respiratory rate, temperature, and mental status every 4 hours
Ensure continuous hydration — clear fluids every 30-60 minutes even if the patient cannot eat
then: Clean wounds daily with boiled and cooled water — watch for redness, swelling, pus, or red streaks
Set up caregiver rotation to prevent fatigue — document all interventions at handoff

title: Mass Casualty Triage — START Protocol
summary: Apply the START triage system to rapidly sort casualties and direct resources where they will save the most lives.

steps.title: The triage principle
steps.body: In a mass casualty incident (MCI), there are more casualties than there are resources to treat them simultaneously. Triage is the process of sorting casualties by treatment priority to achieve the greatest good for the greatest number. It is explicit rationing of care. It requires the uncomfortable decision to delay care to some casualties so that others who are more savable can receive immediate intervention.

steps.title: START categories
steps.body: START = Simple Triage and Rapid Treatment. Four categories: RED (Immediate) — life-threatening but survivable with immediate intervention. YELLOW (Delayed) — serious injury but can wait 30–60 minutes. GREEN (Minor) — walking wounded, can wait hours. BLACK (Expectant/Deceased) — unsurvivable given current resources, or dead. The system uses three assessments: respiration, perfusion, and mental status.

steps.title: Step 1 — Walking wounded
steps.body: Announce loudly: 'If you can walk, move to [designated safe area].' Everyone who walks is GREEN. Tag them and direct them out of the scene. This immediately identifies a large portion of your casualties and removes them from the scene, clearing access to those who cannot ambulate.

steps.title: Step 2 — Assess non-ambulatory casualties
steps.body: For each non-ambulatory casualty in sequence: Check respiration — if not breathing, reposition airway. If still not breathing after repositioning: BLACK. If breathing rate >30 per minute or very slow/gasping: RED. If respiratory rate is within normal: proceed to perfusion check.

steps.title: Step 3 — Perfusion
steps.body: Check for capillary refill (compress the nail bed, release, count seconds to return pink) or radial pulse. Capillary refill >2 seconds or absent radial pulse: RED (hemorrhagic shock). Apply tourniquet or direct pressure immediately, then continue. Capillary refill <2 seconds: proceed to mental status check.

steps.title: Step 4 — Mental status
steps.body: Give simple command: 'Squeeze my hand.' If the casualty cannot follow simple commands: RED. If the casualty can follow simple commands: YELLOW. Complete triage passes through the entire scene tagging each casualty. Then treatment begins, starting with all RED casualties. BLACK patients are triaged expectant — provide comfort if resources later allow, but do not delay care to RED casualties for BLACK casualties.

warnings: Triage is dynamic — a YELLOW patient can become RED and a GREEN can become YELLOW. Reassess all casualties every 15–30 minutes.

Emotionally, tagging a living person BLACK is one of the most difficult actions in emergency medicine. The decision is based on survivability given available resources — it is not abandonment, it is the system working correctly.
proTips: Speed is the purpose of START — the entire system should complete triage of one casualty in under 30 seconds. Individual assessment should not exceed 60 seconds per patient. The goal is to identify all RED casualties as fast as possible.
Tag casualties with color or write on skin with a marker. In dark or smoke-filled environments, verbal relay between team members is required. Never hold a triage category in memory alone.
source: TCCC Guidelines 2023 / CoTCCC / Stop the Bleed
whenNotTo: Do not spend more than 60 seconds assessing any single casualty during initial START triage — speed is the system's purpose
Do not tag a casualty BLACK and move on if the airway has not been repositioned — one repositioning attempt is required before BLACK assignment

quickGlance.situation: You are responding to a mass casualty incident with more casualties than you can treat simultaneously
immediate: Direct all walking wounded to a safe area — everyone who walks is GREEN
Move through remaining casualties: assess respiration, perfusion (capillary refill or radial pulse), and mental status
Tag each casualty RED, YELLOW, GREEN, or BLACK — apply tourniquets to RED casualties and continue
then: Begin treatment starting with all RED casualties
Reassess all casualties every 15-30 minutes — triage categories change as conditions evolve
